entry.title: BW Infocube Compression request taking more disk space
entry.content:
Recently, we completed the BW compression activity for the years DIGITS to DIGITS in the Q system to reduce memory disk space.
However, we have noticed that the disk space has unexpectedly increased compared to the disk usage before the compression activity.
Could you please advise if there are any temporary files, log files, or other system files that can be safely cleaned up to reduce the disk space usage?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: BW Infocube Compression request taking more disk space
entry.content:
From HANA DB poing of view, I think you could regularly archive data as mentioned in this note:

2388483 - How-To: Data Management for Technical Tables
-> /<bw_prefix>/F*

=>
3232938 - [BW Central KBA] How-To: Data Management for BW Infoprovider tables

This is a DB relevant note, however the involved tool/report that is more close to application, you may need contact your BW admin for data archiving.

The meanwhile, I think it is better for you to schedule a redistribution for the whole system in case this has not been performed.

- note
1908075 - SAP BW on SAP HANA: Table Placement and Landscape Redistribution
2334091 - SAP BW/4HANA: Table Placement and Landscape Redistribution

The Redistribution will handle all tables in this system, and this is stongly suggested for BW system.

entry.title: Clarification Required – Joule Premium for Customer Experience Activation Message
entry.content:
Hi SAP Support Team,

We are seeing the following message in SAP for Me:

Joule Premium for Customer Experience – Activation Required

The page shows:

  • AI Units Consumed: 8
  • Entitled Requests: 5,200 per month
  • Excess-use charging may apply if the request limit is exceeded

We are primarily using SAP SuccessFactors and have not intentionally requested activation of the Joule Premium for Customer Experience package.

Please clarify:

  1. Why is this activation message appearing for our account?
  2. Is this package already included in our current contract or entitlement?
  3. Will activating the package result in any additional charges?
  4. How are the 8 AI Units calculated and deducted?
  5. What excess-use charges would apply if the 5,200 monthly requests are exceeded?
  6. Does simply activating the package create any financial commitment?
  7. Is this package relevant to SuccessFactors, or should we use Joule Premium for Human Capital Management instead?

Please review our account entitlement and confirm the commercial impact before we proceed with activation.

 

Thanks,
Aji


--- Steps to Reproduce ---

Hi SAP Support Team,

We are seeing the following message in SAP for Me:

Joule Premium for Customer Experience – Activation Required

We are primarily using SAP SuccessFactors and have not intentionally requested activation of the Joule Premium for Customer Experience package.

Please clarify:

  1. Why is this activation message appearing for our account?
  2. Is this package already included in our current contract or entitlement?
  3. Will activating the package result in any additional charges?
  4. How are the 8 AI Units calculated and deducted?
  5. What excess-use charges would apply if the 5,200 monthly requests are exceeded?
  6. Does simply activating the package create any financial commitment?
  7. Is this package relevant to SuccessFactors, or should we use Joule Premium for Human Capital Management instead?

Please review our account entitlement and confirm the commercial impact before we proceed with activation.

entry.title: Price element on service quotation header
entry.content:
Hi experts,

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for the Service line of business. Our customer has a business scenario that requires adding price conditions at the header level of a service quotation in order to apply a manually entered percentage discount.

However, when we try to add a price element at the header level, the system displays the following warning message:

You cannot add Price Element at header level.

Please see the screenshot below.

Is it not possible to add price conditions at the header level of service quotations and service orders in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, or is this a configurable restriction?

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,

David Avila.

entry.title: In GBI, Are You Marking the Student or the Dataset?
entry.content:
This week, while running the SAP S/4HANA Global Bike (GBI) Train-the-Trainer workshop at SAP Academic Community Conference APAC 2026, I found myself having the same conversation several times during breaks between sessions.

 Its is a conversation I seem to have at almost every SAP academic conference.

Interestingly, many of the academics asking the question were not attending the workshop. Most were already using GBI in their own teaching and were looking for better ways to assess student work at scale.

The question was always some variation of:

"How do I mark students at scale?"

It is a fair question. When you have tens or  hundreds off students completing SAP exercises, manually reviewing every submission quickly becomes impractical.

One thing I have noticed, and was reminded of again this week through conversations with fellow academics, is that many assessment approaches focus on collecting evidence from students.

Students are often asked to submit screenshots, reports, or other evidence showing they completed each step of a process. Academics then review and mark that evidence. I have spoken to academics who spend up to two weeks marking submissions from a cohort of only 60 students.

Others rely heavily on tools such as the student monitor to track activity which, while useful, does have limitations.

Students may make mistakes, restart processes, repeat transactions, or create multiple versions of the same document before arriving at a correct outcome.

In my experience, the student monitor does not always capture these situations particularly well, especially when students restart part of a process after making a mistake.

Which raises an interesting question:

In GBI, are you marking the student or the dataset?

Enterprise systems are designed to generate data. Every sales order, delivery, invoice, accounting document, inventory movement, and customer interaction leaves behind both a business record and a trail showing who created it, when it was created, and how it relates to other enterprise processes.

Take Sales and Distribution (SD) as an example where my focus is on process completion.

For example, I can open an application such as Manage Sales Inquiries, select Go to retrieve all records, and then add fields such as Created By to the report layout.

Within seconds, I have a list of every sales inquiry in the system, key business information about each inquiry and, most importantly, the user who created it.

The same approach can be used for most major business documents in S/4HANA. Sales inquiries, quotations, sales orders, deliveries, billing documents and many other records can all be reported on directly within the system.

The data can then be exported into tools such as Excel, where simple lookups, summary tables and formulas can quickly identify which students (well learn-id) completed which steps within a process. From this I match to the student based on learn Ids.

Over time, I have built reusable spreadsheets that allow me to drop these reports directly into a preconfigured workbook. The formulas, lookups and summary tables do the heavy lifting.

One of the reactions I often get from fellow academics is a mixture of shock and delight when they discover that I can mark large cohorts of up to 600 students in around 30 minutes.

The challenge is rarely the marking itself. The challenge is designing a repeatable process that leverages the data SAP is already generating.

For those interested, I am happy to share the spreadsheets I use for both the SD and MM modules.

Interestingly, before writing this post, I asked an AI how it would assess a typical SD activity at scale.

Its answer was remarkably similar to the approach I have been using for years.

Rather than trying to mark individual transactions, it focused on the relationships between business documents and whether students had successfully completed the process.

Perhaps that should not be surprising.

After all, ERP systems already record the evidence we need for assessment.

entry.title: How to Configure Item Category Determination for Service Transactions
entry.content:
Introduction

When you create a service transaction (such as a service order, service contract, or solution order) and then add an item to it, the system proposes an item category for that item according to your system configuration settings. This process is called item category determination.

The item category controls the behavior of the item in the transaction: how it is processed, priced, billed, and so on. For example, an item category may determine whether a sales item is a standard sales item or a bought-in sales item.

The system determines the item category based on a combination of the following criteria:

  • Service Transaction Type: The type of service transaction, such as solution order, service order, service contract, and so on.
  • Item Category Group: A grouping attribute maintained on the product master that classifies the nature of the product.
  • Item Category Usage: The item category usage is generally used for items that do not refer to a product (such as text items). 
  • Main Item Category: The item category of the higher-level item, relevant when adding sub-items.

For each combination of these criteria, the system looks up a default item category to propose automatically. In addition, one or more alternative item categories can be configured and offered to the user as selectable options.

Configuration Activity for Item Category Determination?

Prior to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608, item category determination was delivered by SAP by default and could not be adjusted by configuration experts.

As of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608, the new configuration activity Change Item Category Determination (ID: 107306) is available.

Customers can use this activity to create more flexible business configurations, for example:

  • Change the default item category used during determination.
  • Remove unnecessary alternative item categories.
  • Adapt service transaction behavior to business requirements.

Key Facts About This Configuration Activity

  • You cannot create new determination entries. SAP owns and delivers the standard content.
  • You can modify values in the default Item Category and Alternative Item Category columns.
  • You can clear an Alternative Item Category field if it should not be offered.
  • The settings apply across the following scope items:
    • 3MO – Service Contract Management
    • 3D2 – Service Order Management and Monitoring
    • 3XK – In-House Repair
    • 4GA – Service Quotation
    • 4GT – Solution Order Management

Typical Use Case

Scenario: Adjust Default Item Category for Service Items in Solution Orders

Background

An IT support company always uses Solution Orders (transaction type SOLO) to handle device repair requests. When a technician adds a service product, the system automatically proposes an item category based on the product's item category group.

SAP delivers the following standard determination: for Solution Orders and Service Product item category group (SRVP), the default item category is Service Item (SOSR). However, the company wants to use a new workflow so that most device repair requests are handled by a subsidiary. Therefore, the business requires the system to propose External Service Item (SOEW) by default, while still allowing users to select SOSR when required.

Service Item SOSR Determined in Solution Order

Step 1: Open Configuration Activity: Change Item Category Determination

As a configuration expert or key user, search for the Change Item Category Determination configuration activity; (ID: 107306) in your configuration environment. Open the configuration activity and you can view a table that lists all the item category determination entries delivered by SAP. Each row represents a combination of criteria (transaction type, item category group, item category usage, main item category) together with a default item category and optional alternative item categories. 

Configuration Activity for Item Category Determination

Step 2: Locate the Relevant Entry

Use the filter or scroll to find the entry that matches your scenario. In our case, we are looking for the determination entry that matches the following attributes:

  • Transaction Type: SOLO
  • Item Category Group: SRVP
  • Item Category Usage: Blank
  • Main Item Category: Blank

Once you locate the entry, you can see the default and alternative item categories that are pre-defined.

Item Category Determination Entry for Solution Order

Step 3: Change the Default Item Category

  1. Select the row to modify.
  2. In the Item Category column, that is designed for the default item category, choose SOEW to replace SOSR. 
  3. In Alternative Item Category 1, choose SOSR to make sure this item category is still available for the end users.
Important: Always select an item category from the value help. The value help list provides you with all the valid item categories for the specific transaction type.
Change Item Category to SOEW

 Step 4: Verify the Configuration in Manage Solution Orders App

As a solution order specialist, you open the Manage Solution Orders app. Create a new Solution Order and add a service item for device repair. The system proposes External Service Item (SOEW) as the default item category, while allowing users to select SOSR from the dropdown list.

Verify Changes in App

Conclusion

With SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608, the Change Item Category Determination configuration activity (ID 107306) provides a modern way to fine-tune item category determination for service transactions.

By understanding the determination criteria:

  • Transaction Type
  • Item Category Group
  • Item Category Usage
  • Main Item Category

Configuration experts can control which item category is proposed by default and which alternatives are available to business users.

This flexibility supports business processes across:

  • Service Orders
  • Service Contracts
  • In-House Repair
  • Service Quotations
  • Solution Orders

I hope this blog help you get started. Feel free to try it in your own configuration environment and share your experiences.

Reference

SAP Help Documentation

entry.title: Re: 信用管理咨询：在未超过信用总额度的情况下，如何基于客户“超期未清项”自动拦截交货单？
entry.content: 客户目前的信用状况如下： 信用总额度： 500,000 CNY 已交货金额（应收账款）： 200,000 CNY（注意：这笔金额已经超期，且客户至今未回款）。 本次计划交货金额： 100,000 CNY 当前总风险敞口： 200,000 + 100,000 = 300,000 CNY（此金额未超过 500,000 CNY 的总额度）。 2. 预期系统行为 (Expected Behavior) 虽然当前的总风险敞口（30万）没有超过客户的信用总额度（50万），但由于客户存在超期未回款的账款（20万），我们希望系统在创建或处理本次 100,000 CNY 的交货单时，能够自动拦截并冻结（Block）单据。 同时，被冻结的交货单需要能够触发审批流（产生信用决策凭证 Credit Decision Document），由信用经理进行手动审批放行或拒绝。

entry.title: Re: Incorrect user during the initial setup
entry.content:
I understand your previous Super Admin is unavailable and you need to assign Super Admin access to the three colleagues you mentioned. For data security, SAP enforces strict controls around S-user creation and Super Admin assignments.
I am not permitted to create new S-users on your behalf when your company already has an active administrative user account. 
 
I reviewed your company account *. Your S-user ID * was a Cloud Admin, and to move this forward I have upgraded it to Super Admin.
You now hold the highest level of permissions with the broadest access rights within your company’s account. 
I also confirmed that the two additional email addresses you shared do not yet have associated S-user IDs under account *. 
 
Next steps for you:
- Use KBA 1271482 - How does an administrator create or delete S-user IDs? - SAP for Meto first create S-user accounts for your other two colleagues.
- Then follow the KBA 1614304 – How to use the Copy Authorization feature for S-user IDs – SAP for Meto copy your authorization permissions to these two newly created S-users. 
 
Once you complete these steps, your organization will have three Super Admins in total.
If anything is unclear or you run into trouble with the KBA steps, please let me know and I will gladly guide you.

entry.title: MCP Server for SAP Incentive Management
entry.content:
Disclaimer

The views and ideas expressed in this blog are strictly my personal views and were built in my personal capacity. This blog does not represent, involve, or reflect the views, architecture, data, systems, security policies, or implementation approach of any organization I currently work for or have worked for in the past.

This blog is intended only as a technical exploration and proof of concept. Any implementation involving SAP Incentive Management, SAP Advanced Workflow, customer environments, backend databases, APIs, AI agents, or MCP servers must be reviewed and approved according to the applicable customer policies, SAP terms, security requirements, data privacy rules, and enterprise architecture standards before being built or used.

Introduction

Enterprise incentive compensation platforms often contain a large amount of operational and analytical data: participants, transactions, credits, earnings, deposits, periods, processing results, and exception scenarios. When a support engineer, consultant, or analyst investigates an issue, the work usually starts with questions such as:

  • Has the transaction data loaded for a participant?
  • Are credits or earnings generated for a given period?
  • What records exist for a specific order, payee, or processing run?
  • Can the result be summarized without manually writing the same SQL repeatedly?

With the rise of AI agents and tool-based reasoning, I wanted to explore whether a natural language interface could help simplify this investigation pattern. This blog presents a personal proof of concept for connecting an MCP server to an SAP Incentive Management backend database, specifically for an Oracle-based application environment, using SAP Advanced Workflow as the controlled access layer.

Core idea: a user asks a data question in natural language, an MCP client invokes a tool, the MCP server calls an SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action, the Web Action performs controlled backend access, and the result is returned to the AI client for tabular display, summarization, or charting.

What Is MCP?

MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is an open protocol that allows AI applications to connect with external tools, systems, and data sources through a standardized client-server model. In MCP, servers expose tools or resources, and AI clients can invoke those tools when needed to answer user questions or perform tasks.

At a high level, MCP includes:

  • Host: the AI application or assistant.
  • Client: the connector inside the host that communicates with an MCP server.
  • Server: the service that exposes tools, data, resources, or actions.
  • Tools: functions that the AI system can call.
  • Resources: contextual data exposed by the server.

Why SAP Incentive Management?

SAP Incentive Management is commonly used to support complex incentive compensation processes, including sales transactions, credits, earnings, payments, disputes, and performance insights. In such systems, data investigation is a recurring activity. A conversational interface can be useful if it is designed carefully, especially for read-only support and analysis scenarios.

Example user questions:

  • Show transactions for participant 1001 for January 2026.
  • Summarize credit records by period.
  • Find failed transaction records for the latest processing period.
  • Display earning results for a participant and explain the output.

Why SAP Advanced Workflow?

SAP Advanced Workflow provides Web Action scripts that can receive external HTTP requests in JSON or XML format and execute workflow-side logic. This makes it an interesting controlled entry point for a proof of concept.

Instead of giving the MCP server direct database connectivity, the pattern I explored uses Advanced Workflow as an intermediate execution layer:

CLI / MCP Client / Conversational AI
        ↓
MCP Server
        ↓
HTTP API Call
        ↓
SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action Script
        ↓
Controlled DB Access Logic
        ↓
SAP Incentive Management Backend Database
        ↓
Result Returned to Advanced Workflow
        ↓
Response Returned to MCP Server
        ↓
AI Agent Formats, Reasons, and Displays Output

This separation matters. The MCP server should not become an uncontrolled database gateway. Advanced Workflow can act as a central place to validate request payloads, enforce allowed operations, and return structured responses.

Proof-of-Concept Scope

For the first version, I kept the implementation intentionally small. The MCP server exposes:

  • A Streamable HTTP MCP endpoint at /mcp.
  • A health endpoint at /health.
  • One read-only MCP tool named select_query.

The initial tool accepts a SQL query and a maximum row count:

{
  "query": "SELECT ...",
  "maxRows": 100
}

The server applies basic read-only restrictions before calling the SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action API:

  • Only SELECT or WITH queries are accepted.
  • Non-read keywords such as INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and DROP are blocked.
  • Multiple statements are blocked.
  • The returned rows are capped using maxRows.

Important Design Warning

For a proof of concept, a read-only select_query tool is useful to validate the integration flow. For a production-grade design, I would not recommend exposing free-form SQL to an AI client, even with keyword blocking. A safer design is to expose a catalog of predefined query types such as participant_transactions, credit_summary, or earning_results, and let the Web Action map those query types to approved SQL statements.

MCP Server and Tool Definition

The MCP server is implemented in Node.js using the MCP SDK. For the blog, I am not including the full server implementation. The core pieces that matter are:

  • The MCP server identity and declared tool capability.
  • The select_query tool definition.
  • The tool request handler that validates input, sanitizes the SQL, calls SAP Advanced Workflow, and returns structured content.

Tool Name and Input Schema

The proof of concept exposes one MCP tool named select_query. The input schema requires a SQL query and optionally accepts a maximum row count.

const SELECT_QUERY_TOOL = "select_query";

const selectQueryInputSchema = z.object({
  query: z.string().min(1).max(10000),
  maxRows: z.number().int().positive().max(5000).optional(),
});

MCP Tool Definition

The tool is described as a read-only operation. The annotations are important because they tell the MCP client that this tool is intended for read-only use and should not be treated as a destructive action.

const tools: Tool[] = [
  {
    name: "select_query",
    title: "Run SELECT query via SAP AWF",
    description:
      "Runs a read-only SQL SELECT query by calling an SAP Advanced Workflow web action endpoint.",
    inputSchema: {
      type: "object",
      properties: {
        query: {
          type: "string",
          description: "Read-only SQL query. Only SELECT/WITH statements are allowed.",
        },
        maxRows: {
          type: "number",
          description: "Optional max rows returned by MCP response.",
        },
      },
      required: ["query"],
      additionalProperties: false,
    },
    annotations: {
      destructiveHint: false,
      openWorldHint: false,
      readOnlyHint: true,
    },
  },
];

MCP Server Definition

The server is registered with a name, version, and tool capability. It supports tool discovery through ListToolsRequestSchema and tool execution through CallToolRequestSchema.

function createMcpServer(): Server {
  const server = new Server(
    { name: "sap-awf-mcp-server", version: "1.0.0" },
    { capabilities: { tools: {} } }
  );

  server.setRequestHandler(ListToolsRequestSchema, async () => ({ tools }));

  server.setRequestHandler(CallToolRequestSchema, async (request: CallToolRequest) => {
    if (request.params.name !== SELECT_QUERY_TOOL) {
      throw new Error(`Unknown tool: ${request.params.name}`);
    }

    const parsedArgs = selectQueryInputSchema.parse(request.params.arguments ?? {});
    const safeQuery = sanitizeSelectQuery(parsedArgs.query);

    const queryResult = await runSelectViaAwf(safeQuery);
    const maxRows = parsedArgs.maxRows ?? appConfig.defaultMaxRows;
    const rows = queryResult.rows.slice(0, maxRows);
    const truncated = queryResult.rows.length > rows.length;

    return {
      content: [
        {
          type: "text",
          text: `Fetched ${rows.length} row(s). Source: SAP Advanced Workflow web action.`,
        },
      ],
      structuredContent: {
        rowCount: queryResult.rows.length,
        returnedRows: rows.length,
        truncated,
        rows,
      },
    };
  });

  return server;
}

SQL Sanitization Before Calling Advanced Workflow

Before sending the query to the Web Action endpoint, the MCP server removes comments, rejects multiple statements, allows only SELECT or WITH, and blocks obvious non-read operations.

function sanitizeSelectQuery(query: string): string {
  const withoutComments = query
    .trim()
    .replace(/--.*$/gm, "")
    .replace(/\/\*[\s\S]*?\*\//g, "")
    .trim();

  if (!withoutComments) {
    throw new Error("Query cannot be empty");
  }

  if ((withoutComments.match(/;/g) || []).length > 1) {
    throw new Error("Only one SQL statement is allowed");
  }

  const normalized = withoutComments.replace(/;\s*$/, "").trim();

  if (!/^(select|with)\b/i.test(normalized)) {
    throw new Error("Only SELECT/WITH queries are allowed");
  }

  const forbiddenOperations =
    /\b(insert|update|delete|merge|drop|alter|truncate|create|grant|revoke|commit|rollback|call|exec(?:ute)?|begin|declare)\b/i;

  if (forbiddenOperations.test(normalized)) {
    throw new Error("Only read-only SELECT queries are allowed");
  }

  return normalized;
}

Security Note on This MCP Design

This server-side validation is useful, but it is still not a complete security boundary. In a mature implementation, I would move from free-form SQL to predefined tools, query allowlists, database views, least-privilege datasource users, row limits, timeouts, audit logging, and field masking.

Configuration Overview

The MCP server uses environment-based configuration so that endpoint URLs and credentials are not hardcoded in source code.

PORT=3100
AWF_WEBACTION_URL=https://<your-awf-host>/api/webactions/<your-script-endpoint>
AWF_HTTP_METHOD=POST
AWF_QUERY_FIELD=query
AWF_TIMEOUT_MS=30000
AWF_ALLOW_INSECURE_TLS=true
DEFAULT_MAX_ROWS=500

# Auth options
# Use either bearer token OR basic auth (username/password), not both.
AWF_BEARER_TOKEN=
AWF_BASIC_USERNAME=<your-awf-username>
AWF_BASIC_PASSWORD=<your-awf-WS-token>
AWF_API_KEY=
AWF_API_KEY_HEADER=x-api-key

# Optional static JSON object merged into each outbound body
# AWF_STATIC_PAYLOAD_JSON={"tenant":"myTenant","script":"runSelect"}
AWF_STATIC_PAYLOAD_JSON=

Some useful fields:

  • AWF_WEBACTION_URL: the Advanced Workflow Web Action endpoint.
  • AWF_QUERY_FIELD: the JSON field expected by the Web Action script for the query.
  • AWF_STATIC_PAYLOAD_JSON: constant fields required by the Web Action script.
  • AWF_BEARER_TOKEN, AWF_BASIC_USERNAME, AWF_BASIC_PASSWORD, AWF_API_KEY: authentication options.
  • AWF_ALLOW_INSECURE_TLS: shown as true for this local proof-of-concept configuration. This should not be used for production unless explicitly approved by security and platform teams.

Advanced Workflow Web Action Script

The Web Action script acts as the controlled bridge between the MCP server and the SAP Incentive Management backend database. In my proof of concept, the Web Action performs four key responsibilities:

  • Read the incoming JSON payload.
  • Extract the SQL query and optional bind values.
  • Validate that the query is read-only.
  • Execute the query through the configured backend datasource and return JSON.

Below is a shortened version of the Web Action logic. I intentionally removed some defensive parsing branches and detailed logging to keep the blog focused.

import groovy.json.JsonOutput
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.transform.Field

@Field String scriptId = 'github_mcp_for_sap_im_db'

response.setContentType('application/json; charset=UTF-8')

try {
    Map payload = readPayload()
    String sqlQuery = extractSql(payload)
    List bindValues = extractBindValues(payload)

    validateReadOnlyQuery(sqlQuery)

    def db = resp.dbConnect('datasource.example')
    List<Map> rows = bindValues
        ? db.queryForList(sqlQuery, bindValues as Object[])
        : db.queryForList(sqlQuery)

    writeJson(200, [success: true, rowCount: rows.size(), rows: rows])
} catch (IllegalArgumentException ex) {
    writeJson(400, [success: false, error: ex.message])
} catch (Exception ex) {
    logger.error(scriptId, "Unexpected query execution error: ${ex.message}")
    writeJson(500, [success: false, error: 'Unable to execute SQL query.'])
}

Map readPayload() {
    String bodyText = request?.getBody()?.toString()
    if (!bodyText?.trim()) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException('Request body is empty.')
    }

    def parsed = new JsonSlurper().parseText(bodyText)
    if (!(parsed instanceof Map)) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException('Request body must be a JSON object.')
    }
    return parsed
}

String extractSql(Map payload) {
    for (String key : ['query', 'sql', 'sqlQuery', 'statement', 'sqlText']) {
        if (payload[key]?.toString()?.trim()) {
            return payload[key].toString().trim()
        }
    }
    throw new IllegalArgumentException('Missing SQL query.')
}

List extractBindValues(Map payload) {
    def values = payload.params ?: payload.values ?: payload.bindValues
    if (values == null) return []
    if (values instanceof List) return values
    if (values instanceof Map) return values.values().toList()
    return [values]
}

void validateReadOnlyQuery(String sqlQuery) {
    String normalized = sqlQuery.trim()
        .replaceAll(/\s+/, ' ')
        .toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT)

    if (normalized.contains(';')) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException('Only one SQL statement is allowed.')
    }

    if (!(normalized.startsWith('select ') || normalized.startsWith('with '))) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException('Only SELECT or WITH statements are allowed.')
    }
}

void writeJson(int statusCode, Map payload) {
    response.setStatus(statusCode)
    response.writer.write(JsonOutput.toJson(payload))
}

Why I Sanitized the Script

The datasource name, endpoint details, table names, and environment-specific configuration should not be published from a real customer or project environment. In the example above, datasource.example is only a placeholder.

The important design behavior is not the exact datasource name. The important behavior is that the Web Action script validates the request, rejects non-read operations, executes only through an approved backend connection, and returns a controlled JSON response.

Example Request Sent to Advanced Workflow

If the Web Action expects the query in a field named query, the MCP server can send a payload like this:

{
  "query": "SELECT * FROM SOME_READ_ONLY_VIEW WHERE ROWNUM <= 10"
}

If the Web Action supports bind values, the MCP server can send a payload like this:

{
  "query": "SELECT * FROM SOME_READ_ONLY_VIEW WHERE PARTICIPANT_ID = ? AND ROWNUM <= ?",
  "params": ["1001", 10]
}

Note: table names and SQL above are illustrative. Do not publish real customer table names, tenant identifiers, endpoint URLs, credentials, screenshots, or production data.

Example User Flow

  1. The user opens an MCP-compatible client, CLI, or AI assistant.
  2. The user asks: “Show me the latest transaction records for participant 1001.”
  3. The AI client decides that it needs data from SAP Incentive Management.
  4. The MCP client invokes the select_query tool on the MCP server.
  5. The MCP server validates that the query is read-only.
  6. The MCP server calls the SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action endpoint.
  7. The Web Action executes controlled backend logic and returns JSON.
  8. The MCP server returns structured data to the client.
  9. The AI agent formats the output as a table, summary, or chart.

Example MCP Tool Response Shape

The MCP server can return both a human-readable text summary and structured content:

{
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "Returned 10 rows from SAP Advanced Workflow."
    }
  ],
  "structuredContent": {
    "rows": [],
    "rowCount": 10,
    "returnedRows": 10
  }
}

This allows the AI client to reason over the data and present it in a business-friendly format.

Important Note: MCP Server as a Gateway, Not the Complete Agent

The MCP server in this proof of concept should be understood as a gateway that allows MCP-capable clients or AI CLI tools to invoke controlled backend functionality through SAP Advanced Workflow. By itself, the MCP server is not a complete business-ready AI agent.

Combining an AI CLI, an MCP client, and an MCP server can create a powerful AI-assisted interaction pattern. However, making it useful for appropriate SAP Incentive Management business scenarios would require significant design work, evaluation, and tuning. Depending on the complexity of the use case, this may require heavy prompt and tool fine-tuning, a stronger semantic layer, predefined business tools, or even a multi-agent approach where different agents handle query planning, validation, business interpretation, data analysis, and response generation.

For SAP Incentive Management scenarios, the goal should not be to let an AI agent freely explore backend data. The goal should be to expose carefully governed, business-safe capabilities that are aligned with approved support, analysis, audit, and reporting use cases.

Security and Governance Considerations

This section is the most important part of the design.

Any architecture that connects AI agents to enterprise compensation data must be treated carefully. Incentive compensation systems can contain sensitive data such as participant information, transaction values, crediting results, earnings, deposits, payment-related values, and dispute information.

1. Validate Customer and SAP Policies

Before building anything similar in a real environment, validate the customer’s policies, SAP SaaS terms, SAP support boundaries, security requirements, data privacy obligations, and enterprise architecture standards.

2. Avoid Arbitrary SQL in Production

Even if the proof of concept blocks non-read keywords, free-form SQL from an AI client is still risky. A stronger production design should use predefined tool operations and approved backend queries.

Riskier pattern:

{
  "query": "SELECT * FROM some_backend_table"
}

Safer pattern:

{
  "queryType": "participant_transactions",
  "participantId": "1001",
  "periodName": "January 2026",
  "limit": 50
}

3. Keyword Blocking Is Not Enough

The Web Action validation shown above blocks semicolons and limits statements to SELECT and WITH. That is useful for a demo, but it is not a complete SQL security model. Production implementations should consider query allowlists, database views, least-privilege datasource users, row limits, execution timeouts, bind variables, and audit logging.

4. Start Read-Only

The first version should be strictly read-only. Do not expose updates, deletes, inserts, compensation recalculation triggers, payment changes, participant updates, or plan configuration changes through an AI tool.

5. Validate Every Input

Validate query type, participant ID, period, row limit, column list, filters, and request size. Do not trust natural language input.

6. Mask Sensitive Output

Mask or remove sensitive data before returning results to the AI client. Depending on customer policy, this may include personal identifiers, payment values, email addresses, employee identifiers, bank-related fields, or other sensitive business data.

7. Enforce Authentication and Authorization

Authentication is required at every hop:

MCP Client → MCP Server
MCP Server → SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action
Advanced Workflow → Backend Data Layer

8. Add Audit Logging

Log who requested what, when, which tool was called, which query type was used, how many rows were returned, and whether the request succeeded or failed.

9. Add Rate Limiting and Timeouts

Protect the Web Action and backend database from excessive calls, large scans, accidental loops, or repeated AI retries.

What I Would Improve Next

The current proof of concept started with one read-only tool. Next improvements could include:

  • Replace free-form SQL with predefined business query tools.
  • Add a metadata endpoint that publishes allowed query types and parameters.
  • Add field-level masking rules.
  • Add a central audit log.
  • Add row-level authorization based on user context.
  • Add response pagination for larger result sets.
  • Add chart-friendly response formats for trend analysis.
  • Add a human approval step for sensitive queries.

Final Thoughts

This proof of concept shows one possible way to connect an AI agent to SAP Incentive Management data using MCP and SAP Advanced Workflow. The valuable part is not just the natural language interface. The valuable part is the controlled integration pattern:

Natural language question
        ↓
MCP tool
        ↓
Controlled Web Action
        ↓
Approved backend logic
        ↓
Structured response
        ↓
AI-assisted explanation

For support and analysis scenarios, this kind of architecture could reduce repetitive investigation effort and make data access more conversational. However, it must be designed with strict governance. Backend database access, AI agents, compensation data, and SaaS environments are not a casual combination.

This blog is only a personal technical exploration and proof of concept. Any real implementation must go through customer approval, SAP policy validation, security review, architecture review, and operational readiness assessment.

References

If the SAP Community editor does not preserve clickable links after pasting HTML, paste these references as plain URLs or use the editor's link button manually.

  1. Model Context Protocol Specification
    https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-03-26
  2. Model Context Protocol Basic Protocol Overview
    https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/basic
  3. SAP Help Portal: Web Action Script Example
    https://help.sap.com/docs/Advanced_Workflow/b7cb13f8b6a04157a722ad9ddfde4a28/72b77b7c7c231014a804993ce4041860.html
  4. SAP Community: Advanced Workflow White Paper - Web Actions
    https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-spm-incentive-management-advanced-workflow-white-paper-web-actions/ba-p/14014302
  5. SAP Help Portal: SAP Commissions
    https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_Commissions

Closing Question for the SAP Community

I would be interested to hear from the SAP Community: what are safer patterns for connecting AI agents to enterprise compensation systems, and where should the boundary be between conversational access, approved APIs, reporting tools, and backend-level investigation?

entry.title: Adding an inbuilt Translation view in C4C using SAP Translation Hub service
entry.content:
Introduction

In multilingual business environments, service agents frequently receive customer emails written in languages other than their own. For example, a German‑speaking customer service representative may receive inquiries in French or Swedish. To understand the customer’s issue and provide an appropriate response, agents often rely on external translation tools. This requires translating the incoming email, taking action based on its content, drafting a reply in their own language, and then translating the response back into the customer’s preferred language.
This multi‑step process is time‑consuming, disrupts workflow, and negatively impacts overall user experience and productivity.

 

To streamline this process and enhance user efficiency, we aim to provide an integrated translation capability directly within SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C). By leveraging the SAP Translation Hub service available on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), users will be able to translate incoming and outgoing messages without navigating to external tools.
This built-in solution will significantly reduce manual effort, improve turnaround time, and deliver a seamless user experience for agents supporting multilingual customer interactions.

Architecture

Below is high level architecture that we will follow-

  • BTP app (Node.js or CAP) exposes /api/translate → calls SAP Translation Hub REST API using an OAuth token (Destination service + XSUAA).
  • Simple web UI (UI5/HTML) lets the user choose source text/language and target language; it calls /api/translate, shows the result.
  • C4C  URL Mashup embeds the web UI.

         

 

 

 

Step 1 — Provision SAP Translation Hub on BTP

a) In BTP Cockpit, entitle and create a service instance of SAP Translation Hub, Free‑tier quotas are available for trying this out.

 

 

You are now able to create service bindings for your new service instance. Service bindings are used to generate credentials to enable apps to access and communicate with the service instance.

 

In the dialog, enter a binding name, such as binding01 the name of your service binding. Click Create button to create the service binding.

 

Download this file and copy values as highlighted above. They will be used in next step while creating destination.

clientid, clientsecret, url (API endpoint), and uaa.url (token endpoint).

 

b) Review the API access model (OAuth 2.0 client credentials) and the Translate method in the API reference.

 API Reference - Software Translation | SAP Help Portal

c) On api.sap.com, you can explore SAP Translation Hub and Document Translation endpoints and their request/response shapes.

Overview | SAP Translation Hub | SAP Business Accelerator Hub 

 

Step 2 — Create a BTP Destination for Translation Hub

Create a destination (e.g., STH_REST) so your backend can call Translation Hub without hard‑coding secrets:

Go to Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination.

 

Using destinations centralizes credentials and lets the Cloud SDK or approuter acquire tokens at runtime.

 

Note:

  • Use the uaa.url value from the service key plus /oauth/token appended — e.g. https://xxxxxxx.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token.
  • Common mistake: pasting the bare uaa.url without /oauth/token, or using the url field (the API endpoint) instead of uaa.url (the auth endpoint) — these are two different keys in the service key JSON and easy to swap.
  • Set Token Service URL Type to Dedicated (not Common) unless you're deliberately sharing a token across multiple destinations.

 

Step 3 — Implement a tiny backend that calls Translation Hub

 

Below is a minimal Node.js (Express) route using the SAP Cloud SDK to call Translation Hub via the destination you created. (You can also do this in CAP; the principle is the same.)


Note: The exact payload of translate (fields like sourceLanguage, targetLanguages, textType, domain, units) is documented in the API ref; adapt the body to the Translate Method for API v2

 

// server.js
import express from 'express';
import cors from 'cors';
import { executeHttpRequest } from '@sap-cloud-sdk/http-client';

const app = express();
app.use(express.json());
app.use(cors());	

app.post('/api/translate', async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const { sourceLanguage, targetLanguage, text, domain, textType } = req.body;

    // Build API payload per SAP Translation Hub "translate" method (API v2)
    const payload = {
      sourceLanguage,
      targetLanguages: [targetLanguage],
      // optional depending on your use case:
      domain,        // e.g., "FIN", "CRM" – see “domains” method
      textType,      // e.g., "ui", "longtext" – see “texttypes” method
      units: [{ value: text }]
    };

    const response = await executeHttpRequest(
      { destinationName: 'STH_REST' }, // BTP Destination
      {
        method: 'POST',
        url: '/v2/translate',          // adjust if your base URL already includes /v2
        data: payload,
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }
      }
    );

    // Typical response has translations aligned to the requested targetLanguages
    res.json(response.data);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
    res.status(500).json({ error: 'Translation failed', details: `${err}` });
  }
});

const port = process.env.PORT || 4000;
app.listen(port, () => console.log(`Translation backend running on ${port}`));

 

Step 4 — Build a simple web UI (UI5 or plain HTML)

A small form that posts to /api/translate is enough. You can deploy the static UI and backend together to Cloud Foundry behind an approuter (HTML5 repo) and expose a single HTTPS URL.

 

<!-- public/index.html -->
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8"/>
  <title>Translate (SAP Translation Hub)</title>
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1"/>
  <style>
    body {
      font-family: "Arial", sans-serif;
      font-size: 14px;
      line-height: 1.4;
      padding: 20px;
      max-width: 800px;
      margin: 0 auto;
      color: #333;
    }
    h2 { margin-top: 0; }
    .form-group { margin-bottom: 15px; }
    label { display: inline-block; margin-right: 15px; font-weight: bold; }
    
    /* Updated styling for select and inputs */
    input, select { 
      padding: 6px; 
      border: 1px solid #ccc; 
      border-radius: 4px; 
      background-color: white;
    }
    
    textarea {
      width: 100%;
      padding: 8px;
      border: 1px solid #ccc;
      border-radius: 4px;
      box-sizing: border-box;
      resize: vertical;
    }
    button {
      background-color: #0070f2;
      color: white;
      border: none;
      padding: 10px 20px;
      border-radius: 4px;
      cursor: pointer;
      font-size: 14px;
    }
    button:hover { background-color: #005bb5; }
    button:disabled { background-color: #ccc; cursor: not-allowed; }
    #out {
      background: #f4f4f4;
      padding: 15px;
      border-radius: 4px;
      border: 1px solid #ddd;
      white-space: pre-wrap;
      margin-top: 20px;
      min-height: 50px;
    }
    .error { color: #d93025; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <h2>Translate</h2>

  <div class="form-group">
    <label>Source language:
      <select id="srcLang">
        <option value="en" selected>English</option>
        <option value="zh">Chinese (Simplified)</option>
        <option value="es">Spanish</option>
        <option value="ar">Arabic</option>
        <option value="pt">Portuguese</option>
        <option value="fr">French</option>
        <option value="ja">Japanese</option>
        <option value="ru">Russian</option>
        <option value="de">German</option>
        <option value="hi">Hindi</option>
      </select>
    </label>
    
    <label>Target language:
      <select id="tgtLang">
        <option value="en">English</option>
        <option value="zh">Chinese (Simplified)</option>
        <option value="es">Spanish</option>
        <option value="ar">Arabic</option>
        <option value="pt">Portuguese</option>
        <option value="fr">French</option>
        <option value="ja">Japanese</option>
        <option value="ru">Russian</option>
        <option value="de" selected>German</option>
        <option value="hi">Hindi</option>
      </select>
    </label>
  </div>

  <div class="form-group">
    <textarea id="srcText" rows="5" placeholder="Enter text to translate..."></textarea>
  </div>

  <button id="go" type="button">Translate</button>

  <pre id="out"></pre>

  <script>
    // Read initial values from URL params
    const qp = new URLSearchParams(location.search);
    const srcTextInput = document.getElementById('srcText');
    const tgtLangInput = document.getElementById('tgtLang');
    const srcLangInput = document.getElementById('srcLang');
    const goBtn = document.getElementById('go');
    const outPre = document.getElementById('out');

    // Initialize fields from URL if present
    if (qp.get('text')) srcTextInput.value = qp.get('text');
    
    // If URL params provide a language code that exists in our list, select it
    if (qp.get('targetLang')) tgtLangInput.value = qp.get('targetLang');
    if (qp.get('sourceLang')) srcLangInput.value = qp.get('sourceLang');

    goBtn.addEventListener('click', async () => {
      // Reset output and set loading state
      outPre.textContent = '';
      outPre.classList.remove('error');
      goBtn.disabled = true;
      const originalBtnText = goBtn.textContent;
      goBtn.textContent = 'Translating...';

      try {
        const body = {
          sourceLanguage: srcLangInput.value,
          targetLanguage: tgtLangInput.value,
          text: srcTextInput.value,
          domain: qp.get('domain') || undefined,
          textType: qp.get('textType') || 'ui'
        };

        const response = await fetch('/api/translate', {
          method: 'POST',
          headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
          body: JSON.stringify(body)
        });

        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error(`Server error: ${response.status} ${response.statusText}`);
        }

        const data = await response.json();
        outPre.textContent = JSON.stringify(data, null, 2);

      } catch (error) {
        console.error('Translation failed:', error);
        outPre.textContent = `Error: ${error.message}`;
        outPre.classList.add('error');
      } finally {
        // Restore button state
        goBtn.disabled = false;
        goBtn.textContent = originalBtnText;
      }
    });
  </script>
</body>
</html>

 

Step 5 — Deploy the app to BTP (Cloud Foundry)

 

  • Push the Node.js app (and static UI) to your CF space.
  • Ensure the Destination is bound/available.
  • Note the public URL (e.g., https://your-app.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/).
  • The workshop’s sample shows the same flow (basic website + microservice) jens-limbach/SSv2-extensibility-workshop: SSv2-extensibility-workshop

 

Step 6 — Create the URL Mashup in C4C  and place it on a page

 

In C4C, go to Mashup Authoring → Create → URL Mashup.

          URL: paste your BTP app URL

Place the mashup: open a record (e.g., Ticket), Start Adaptation, add a new tab/section, drop the mashup, save. Here is the preview of the new mash up. I have used very basic HTML logic to create view; further modification can be done in HTML logic to blend in the view with C4C theme.

Note: SAP C4C V2 provides native, AI‑powered language translation. If needed this can be used in C4C V2 as well.

 

Conclusion

We have explored how SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) can be enhanced with built-in translation capabilities using SAP Translation Hub on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). By implementing this solution, organizations can provide a seamless translation experience directly within C4C, enabling service agents to efficiently manage multilingual customer interactions, improve productivity, and deliver faster, more effective support.

I hope this guide helps you understand the end-to-end implementation approach and inspires further innovation in multilingual customer service scenarios. Should you have any questions, require clarification on any of the implementation steps, or wish to discuss alternative design approaches, please feel free to reach out. Your feedback and suggestions are highly valued and will help refine and strengthen this solution for broader adoption and future enhancements.

entry.title: Re: S/4 HANA OUTPUT MANAGEMENT USING BRF+ For Settlement Document in 2022 On-Premises editions
entry.content: Thanks for nice illustration!

entry.title: Breaking Down Silos to Unified Customer Data — Powered by the Advanced Success Plan for SAP CX
entry.content:
More Tools, More Fragmentation

Each day, businesses invest in new software tools: marketing platforms, commerce engines, service systems, and sales technology. They are told that assembling the right combination will unlock their digital transformation journey and finally deliver that personalized and seamless experience they wish to provide to their customers.

But what if more or better software is not necessarily the answer?

Each solution solves a real problem for a specific team. But collectively, they create a consequence no one planned for: every new tool builds its own data world, without a common language or context across them. In a 2026 study by Oxford Economics, only 25% of respondents described their CX technology environment as fully harmonized and integrated. 29% remain highly fragmented. Organizations with siloed CX tech are more likely to face an inability to connect customer needs to actionable data insights. 58% reported this challenge, compared to 47% among those with harmonized environments.

More than what tools businesses choose to add to their CX landscape, how they connect and interact with each other becomes even more important. A unified data strategy sounds straightforward in principle. In practice, most businesses find that the obstacle is not ambition but rather execution. Every integration decision made without a clear data architecture becomes a future campaign mired in manual reconciliation, a customer journey that breaks at the handoff, or a personalization promise the disparate data sources cannot support. Implementation without a validated strategy creates new fragmentation inside the solution meant to eliminate the old. And without a structured way to pressure-test decisions before any commitment is made, even well-resourced organizations find themselves repeating the same cycle: invest, integrate, fragment, repeat.

A Better Way to Build

The real barrier is not budget or technology.

A Forrester study of 1,000+ senior executives found that data quality and integration issues are cited as the top cause (39%) of delayed or derailed transformation projects, more than any other factor such as budget, technology maturity, and talent. The same study found that 56% of respondents struggle with poor data quality; 55% face persistent data silos. This is not for lack of investment in technology, but because the connections between systems were not designed or maintained effectively.

What bridges that gap is not another platform — it is the expertise to think through data connectivity decisions before they are made, and the ongoing guidance to ensure those decisions compound into measurable gains over time. The Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience solutions provides that guidance along every step of the journey.

Define what success actually looks like

The most common reason data unification projects fall short of expectations is not technical failure – it is a failure to define and measure what success looks like for the entire business before the work begins. The Value Management session from the Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience establishes that definition at the outset: what does a fully unified data strategy actually enable? It means running the next marketing campaign without manual data reconciliation, and presenting an AI readiness roadmap without caveats. Stakeholders will know, at every checkpoint, whether the investment is moving the business forward, not just moving the project forward.

Design the strategy before building the integrations

A robust data strategy starts with good design. Product Guidance from the Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience covers available out-of-the-box integrations, common usage scenarios, and pitfalls and how to avoid them — all delivered in a live remote session by an SAP expert who can answer questions in real time. The data strategy can be conceptualized and pressure-tested before any budget or technical commitments are made.

Validate every critical decision with expert guidance

With the Technical Assistance and Functional Assistance from the Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience, businesses have continuous access to expert guidance at every critical decision point. Beyond resolving immediate questions, the ongoing access also shares insight on how SAP thinks through problems, strengthening in-house expertise with every interaction. The result is an organization that makes better decisions not just now but for the future.

Measure whether the strategy is delivering

Adoption and Innovation Checkpoints conducted on a quarterly or semi-annual basis bring the measurement back to where it started: the business outcomes defined at the outset. Do campaigns run without manual reconciliation? Is the AI use case performing against its stated goals? A clear throughline from the Value Management success KPIs to the Adoption and Innovation Checkpoints proves the benefits of the investment.

Where cycles and silos break

The cycle of invest, integrate, fragment, repeat is not inevitable. It is the predictable result of making technical decisions without an anchoring business imperative, and integration decisions without strategic expert guidance. Organizations that break the cycle do not necessarily have better technology than their competitors — they have better judgment about how to use it. The Advanced Success Plan for SAP Customer Experience exists for exactly that reason: to put proactive and prescriptive guidance at every decision point where that judgment matters most. When data strategy is designed before it is built, validated before it is committed, and measured against real business outcomes, the technology investment already made starts working harder. The stack was never the problem. When the thinking behind the technology finally matches its ambition, the personalized, seamless experience becomes a reality.

Where is your organization in the invest, integrate, fragment, repeat cycle? What would it require to break the cycle organizationally, not just technically?

entry.title: Linked POBs
entry.content:
A linked performance obligation (POB) is an implicitly promised item. It is used when you want an item in RA contract which doesn’t have a corresponding item in the sales document. This blog will act as a guide to the creation of a linked POB. It includes the exact configuration steps and price allocation among POBs with a detailed example. 

 A linked POB is created in reference to a leading POB. There are two ways to create a Linked POB:

BRF+ configuration

In Decision Table Process add. POB

Example:

Created an order with 2 line items in SD

In RAIMON, we see two POBs

In contract, we see there’s a third POB created wrt the first POB

POB structure UI

Adding a linked POB using NWBC

Price Allocation

Scenario 1: Where SSP is not defined for the linked POB

Scenario 2: Where SSP is defined for the Linked POB

To note:

In optimized Contract Management, the leading POB cannot be time-based.​

Examples:

  1. A software license along with installation service. Installation is an implicitly promised item along with a software, which if not included in a contract explicitly is needed as a linked POB

Software license- Leading POB
Installation service- Linked POB

2. A water filter and Annual maintenance service with it which isn’t a separate line in a contract is a linked POB with the water filter as the leading POB

Water filter- Leading POB
Annual Maintenance service- Linked POB

Conclusion

To conclude, Linked POB is a useful feature which can be setup easily with the right approach. I hope the configuration steps and allocation logic covered in the post help you with your implementation. 

Do let me know in the comments below if you have any questions about linked POBs!

P.S. This isn't an AI-generated content.

entry.title: Custom Fields with Field Status Group control in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
One of the most significant changes introduced by SAP S/4HANA is the unification of financial tables into a single source of truth: the Universal Journal, or table ACDOCA. With this new table, creating new fields is no longer an exclusive task for ABAP developers. Through SAP Fiori, functional consultants now have access to an application that allows us to create custom fields and assign them to Field Status Groups.

Depending on the intended business use of the field, it can be populated automatically using a BAdI (BADI_FINS_ACDOC_POSTING_EVENTS) or manually during journal entry posting.

In this blog, I will explain through a practical example how to create a custom field available for data entry during General Journal Entries posting. The allowed values for this field will be restricted by a drop-down list (Code List), and its field status requirement (Required/Optional/Hidden) will be controlled by the Field Status Group.

Creating the Custom Field

To create a custom field, we must use the SAP Fiori app Custom Fields (App ID: F1481😞

Upon opening the app, select the "Create" (plus icon) button located in the upper right corner:

A pop-up dialog will appear with the following fields:

Since the new field must be available for data entry when creating a G/L journal entry, you must select FINS_CODING_BLOCK in the Business Context field:

Enter the label for the new field and select the field type from the drop-down list. For this exercise, this custom field should only be populated during posting using specific values, so we select the Code List option. Selecting this option enables new configuration fields within the pop-up to define the drop-down values:

  • Length: Defines the character length of the drop-down values during journal entry creation, which directly determines the technical length of the custom field.
  • List Values: Use this section to manage the dropdown values and their corresponding descriptions.

After filling in all required fields, select the Create button at the bottom right of the pop-up window. To publish the field, navigate to the details page:

Important: Since this field will be controlled via the Field Status Group (Transaction OBC4), it is not necessary to enable user interfaces.

To finalize the field creation process, proceed to publish it by clicking the Publish button at the bottom right of the screen. Once published, the field is successfully created and will exist within table ACDOCA:

Including the Field in the Coding Block

This activity must be performed via SAP GUI, either by navigating through SPRO to the Customizing activity Edit Coding Block or by directly executing transaction code OXK3:

First and foremost, this activity allows the inclusion of custom fields into FI General Ledger accounts, enabling them within the Field Status Groups. It also allows fields to be integrated into MM Stock Management, MM Purchasing, and CO Line Items.

For our example, this process will enable the new custom field on an FI subscreen, making it visible and available during journal entry posting.

Upon accessing this customizing activity, the transaction will display two options. We must select Customer–Defined Account Assignments, and within it, Customer Include Structure. This is where the CI_COBL structure is located, which we will use to link our newly created custom field:

To add the field, double-click on the CI_COBL structure and populate only the Component and Component Type fields using the exact field identifier generated in the Custom Fields SAP Fiori app:

Proceed to save the changes. Then, double-click on the structure once again to activate the new field:

Once the activation process is executed, an activation log will be generated where you can review any occurring errors. Typically, two errors are displayed: one related to table COEP and another related to table MSEG:

To resolve the error associated with table COEP, follow the instructions provided in KBA 2384731 - Correction program: COEP and replacement object V_COEP have different number of columns. This SAP Note will instruct you to execute the report FCO_CDS_VIEW_GENERATE. Once this program has run successfully, the new custom field will be visible in the V_COEP view:

To resolve the error associated with table MSEG, follow the instructions provided in KBA 2242679 - Redirect inconsistency - Proxy Substitution. The assistance of an ABAP developer will be required to fix this error. Since this is a walkthrough example, we can ignore the error in table MSEG and proceed; however, it is critical to always resolve all occurring errors to prevent future data inconsistencies in the production environment.

Creating the Coding Block Subscreen

Once the errors have been resolved and the CI_COBL structure is fully activated, the account assignment subscreen must be created. This configuration activity can be accessed either via transaction code OXK3 or by directly executing transaction OXK1.

Upon executing the transaction, select the "Create" (New) option. The system will prompt you to create a new subscreen number within the allowed custom range of 9000 – 9999:

The new field will be available, and you will only need to assign a position number to it and generate the screen:

The new subscreen will be created under the Customer Subscreens section:

If the new custom field is missing from the subscreen you are creating, follow the recommendations provided in KBA 3609989 - Added Z Coding Block Field Not Available on OXK1 Screen.

Updating the Field Status Group

This is the final activity required to complete the end-to-end setup. The first task that must be performed—not due to a strict process sequence, but because it is commonly overlooked—is to change the field status of the new custom field within the Posting Keys that will be used for the corresponding accounts. This is maintained via transaction OB41 (Posting Keys). For this exercise, the changes were applied only to posting keys 40 (Debit) and 50 (Credit).

The second task is to update the Field Status Variant where the field status group assigned to the target G/L accounts is located. This configuration activity can be accessed via the SPRO reference IMG menu path or by directly executing transaction code OBC4:

Once inside the variant and the specific field status group, you must select the "Additional account assignments" field group:

If the technical name of the new field appears as "Subgroup text is missing", you must follow the resolution steps outlined in KBA 3592675 - OBC4: the short name is "Subgroup text is missing":

Once the steps suggested in KBA 3592675 are completed, the field name will be displayed correctly. You can then proceed to select the required field status (e.g., Optional or Required entry) and save the changes:

Testing with a Journal Entry Document

Once all system preparation and customizing activities are completed, we can post financial documents using the new custom field.

For our example, we will execute the SAP Fiori app Post General Journal Entries (App ID: F0718) and select a G/L account that has the modified field status group assigned within its Master Data. Within the line item details, select the "More" subscreen button, where you will see the new custom field available with its drop-down list:

Conclusion

Throughout this document, we've seen that this approach to managing custom fields is specific to general ledger postings. Using custom fields in other modules might require ABAP support. We've verified that we can manage the creation of these fields from start to finish and even enable them in field status groups. I hope this post is helpful for all functional consultants who enjoy exploring the limits of SAP.

entry.title: Field Validation in SAP SuccessFactors Performance Management Using Business Rules
entry.content:
During a recent SuccessFactors Performance Management implementation, a customer wanted to ensure that managers provide comments whenever an employee receives a low performance rating. This requirement was addressed using Business Rules to validate the performance form before submission.

Business Requirement:

In one of our SAP SuccessFactors Performance Management implementations, the customer wanted managers to rate only 3 competencies out of a list of 10+ competency items during the Manager Assessment step.

If a manager rated fewer than 3 competencies or more than 3 competencies, the system should display an error message and prevent the form from moving forward.

Solution:

To address this requirement, we leveraged the Get Count of Ratings function available in SAP SuccessFactors Business Rules.
The rule was configured to count the number of competency ratings provided by the manager within the Competency Section. The validation was performed on:

Form.Form Section.Competencies.Official Rating

Since the rating scale ranged from 1 to 5, any competency with a rating greater than 0 was considered as a rated competency.
The business rule counted the total number of rated competencies and validated whether the count was exactly 3.

Error Message:

"Please rate exactly 3 competencies before submitting the performance form."

A key challenge was determining when the validation should be triggered. If the rule was configured solely based on the rating count, the error message would appear throughout the entire performance review process, starting from the initial form stage.

To avoid this behavior, we used option to define the stage:
 Form.Route Map.Current Step 

This allowed us to restrict the validation to the specific Manager Assessment step, ensuring that the error message was displayed only when the manager was expected to complete the competency ratings.

Benefits:

This approach is particularly useful when performance forms contain a large number of competencies or evaluation items.

Key benefits include:

  • Ensures compliance with business requirements.
  • Improves consistency in manager evaluations.
  • Prevents overrating or underrating competency selections.

Conclusion:

Using the Get Count of Ratings function provides a simple and effective way to enforce rating limits within SAP SuccessFactors Performance Management. Business Rules provide an efficient way to enforce validation requirements in Performance Management without custom development. Properly designed rules help organizations improve compliance and maintain high-quality performance review data.

entry.title: Re: ASN quantity in Cumulative received Qty in the inboun...
entry.content:
Do not overwrite SAP’s cumulative delivered qty with customer’s cumulative received qty. Instead:

  • Keep SAP standard logic intact (PGI-driven).

  • Store customer’s cumulative received qty in a custom field/report.

  • Use reconciliation reports to bridge the difference.

This way, you avoid breaking SAP’s scheduling agreement logic while still honoring the customer’s reporting requirement.

entry.title: Banking Payments paid Invoices posting dates of Invoices Query
entry.content:
Hi All

Please guide me to join INV1 to get GL Account Number in this Query.

 

SELECT T0.DocNum, T0.JrnlMemo, T0.DocType, T0.Canceled, T0.DocDate, T0.CardCode, T0.DocCurr, T0.DocTotal, T0.DocTotalFC, T2.DocNum as 'Invoice Number', T2.DocDate as 'Invoice Date', T2.JrnlMemo, T2.DocTotal as 'Original Invoice Amount', T3.DocNum as 'Credit Note Number', T3.DocTotal as 'Original Credit Note Amount', T4.TransId as 'JE Transaction Number', T3.JrnlMemo,  T1.InvoiceId, T1.DocEntry, (Case when T1.InvType = '14' then (T1.SumApplied * -1) else T1.SumApplied end) as 'Sum Applied', T1.SumApplied, T1.AppliedFC, T1.InvType, (case when T1.InvType= '30' then 'JE' when T1.InvType = '13' then 'AR Inv' when T1.InvType = '14' then 'AR Inv' when T1.InvType = '24' then 'RC' else '' end) as 'DocType',  T1.DocLine FROM ORCT T0 INNER JOIN RCT2 T1 ON T0.DocEntry = T1.DocNum LEFT JOIN OINV T2 ON T2.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry and T1.InvType = T2.ObjType

LEFT JOIN ORIN T3 ON T3.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry and T1.InvType = T3.ObjType

LEFT JOIN OJDT T4 ON T4.TransId = T1.DocEntry and T1.InvType = T4.ObjType

WHERE T0.Canceled = 'N' and T0.DocDate between [%0] and [%1] and T2.DocDate <[%2]

 

 

Kedalene chong

entry.title: Re: How to validate manually entered batch numbers during...
entry.content:
Alternative approaches you can consider:

1) Number Range Control (Configuration)
Use external number ranges for batches and restrict manual entry to those ranges. This ensures that any manually entered batch number outside the defined range will be rejected by standard checks.

2) Validation via Custom Fields + Logic.
Instead of validating the batch number directly during creation, introduce a custom field (e.g., “Batch Validation Flag”) and implement validation logic in Custom Logic that checks your rules. You can then raise warnings or errors indirectly by controlling follow-on processes (e.g., goods movement posting).

3) Process Restriction
If strict validation is mandatory, consider disabling manual batch entry and enforce automatic batch number assignment with controlled number ranges. This avoids the need for manual validation altogether.

entry.title: Re: SAP Signavio - Object Centric View- Issue with Events are not appearing in Process Discovery
entry.content:
Hi Jai_Jain,

I've run into something similar while setting up OCPM in O2C. A few common causes worth checking, in case one applies to your setup:

  • Case scope filtering events out – If case scope was defined narrowly (e.g., tied to a specific object type or a date/status filter), any event that isn't linked to an object within that scope won't surface in discovery, even if the event itself is correctly modeled. Worth re-checking whether the scope excludes valid variants.
  • Object–event linkage in the semantic model – In OCPM, an event only shows up in discovery if it's correctly linked to at least one in-scope object via the relevant qualifier/role. If an event references an object type that wasn't fully mapped (or the foreign key/relationship wasn't captured during network discovery), it gets silently dropped rather than flagged as an error.
  • Thresholds/filters on the discovery view itself – Separate from data issues, Process Discovery often applies a default frequency or variant threshold. Low-frequency events can be hidden from the graph even though they exist in the underlying log. Worth resetting thresholds to zero to confirm this isn't just a display issue.
  • Source data completeness in Process Network Discovery – Since network discovery infers relationships automatically, it can miss weaker or indirect object relationships (e.g., events connected only through an intermediate object). If that link wasn't picked up, events tied only through that path won't appear.

My experience as to the fastest way to debug: grab the raw log for one of the missing events, check if it has a valid object reference, and see if that object type is actually inside your case scope. Hope that helps.

entry.title: Monthly Benefit Carry Forward with Hire Date Proration
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

While configuring a Reimbursement benefit with the frequency set to Monthly and Balance Carry Forward enabled, is there a way to prorate the Entitlement Amount only for the month in which the employee joins the organization, and have the benefit continue with the full monthly entitlement from the following month onward?

I am aware of the Benefits Exception feature. However, in organizations with frequent mid-month joiners, maintaining exceptions becomes labor-intensive and prone to errors, especially since the entitlement amount varies based on the employee's Pay Grade and Benefit Type.

I tried implementing this through a business rule, but it is prorating the Entitlement Amount for every month as long as the employee's hire date falls within the current benefit cycle (April to March).

For example, if an employee's monthly entitlement is ₹20,000 and they joined on 10 June 2026, the rule currently calculates the entitlement as ₹28,000 (₹14,000 for June + ₹14,000 for July). However, the expected entitlement should be ₹34,000, where ₹14,000 is the prorated entitlement for June and ₹20,000 is the full entitlement for July.

Has anyone implemented a similar requirement or found a standard approach to achieve this?

Thanks,
Mohd Saif

entry.title: Re: Teaching SAP Without Teaching SAP
entry.content:
Hi, Matt,

Thank you for this very valuable perspective. I really resonate with the idea that we shouldn’t start by asking, “How do we teach SAP?” but rather, “How does SAP help us better teach the concepts that students already need to understand?”

In my experience, especially in the areas of accounting, management information systems, and ERP systems, SAP becomes an extremely useful tool for transforming abstract concepts into concrete learning experiences. Students can see how a sales order affects delivery, invoicing, accounting, inventory, and management reporting. At that point, concepts such as integrated processes, traceability, internal control, segregation of duties, and data-driven decision-making are no longer just theoretical definitions.

I, too, believe that the main value lies not in memorizing SAP transactions, but in students understanding how a real organization works. When they work in an ERP system, they don’t just learn about business processes—they directly experience the connections between departments, data, documents, and decisions.

In my courses, SAP is used to bridge the gap between theory and practice: accounting, sales, procurement, production, projects, reporting, and automation. Students gain a better understanding of why data quality matters, why controls are necessary, why processes need to be standardized, and how technology supports the operations of modern organizations.

I would say that, often, students end up learning SAP precisely because they are trying to understand something else: a process, a business problem, a managerial decision, or a control situation. And this is probably the best justification for integrating SAP into the curriculum.

For me, SAP isn’t just a software platform taught to students, but an applied learning environment where concepts become visible, relevant, and connected to economic reality.

Thank you for starting this discussion. I think it’s a very important topic for all of us who are trying to bring university education closer to real-world practice in organizations.

 

Viorel BANTA

Bucharest University of Economic Studies, Romania

#SAP Academic Partnerships #CIG-IG #SAP
